title: Programmable Gates and Data Routing
summary: See how control bits, encoders, decoders, multiplexers, and demultiplexers route digital data.
content_html:
Some gates can be used as controlled building blocks. XOR can behave like a controlled inverter: when the control input is 0, the output follows A; when the control input is 1, the output becomes A'. AND can behave like a logic-controlled switch: when the control input is 0, the output is forced to 0; when the control input is 1, the data input passes through.

XOR controlled inverter:
C = 0 -> Out = A
C = 1 -> Out = A'

AND controlled switch:
A = 0 -> Out = 0
A = 1 -> Out = B

Digital systems also need circuits that route and convert binary signals. An encoder converts one active input line into a coded binary output. A decoder does the reverse: it uses a binary input code to select one output line.

A multiplexer selects one of many input lines and sends it to one output. A demultiplexer sends one input line to one of many outputs. These circuits are common in memory, communication systems, displays, keyboards, and data buses.

exercises.title: Controlled inverter
exercises.prompt: Which gate can act as a controlled inverter when paired with a control input?

exercises.title: Decoder role
options: Uses a binary input code to select one output line
Converts all inputs to Python variables
exercises.answer: Uses a binary input code to select one output line

exercises.title: Many to one
exercises.prompt: Which circuit selects one input from many possible input lines and sends it to a single output?

exercises.title: Priority encoder
exercises.prompt: What is the key behavior of a priority encoder when multiple input lines are high?
options: It encodes the highest-priority active input
It adds all active inputs together
It turns every output line on
exercises.answer: It encodes the highest-priority active input
